nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Dilip, Anirban, Gopal, Alok, Anindya, Arunava, Asit, Asok, Dibyendu, Goutam, Indra, Indranil.

variants.name: Dey
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Ashit, Indu, Naina, Partha, Soumya, Ajit, Anjan, Anjana, Anup, Arjun, Arun, Ashim.
English, Scottish, and Welsh: variant of   Day  .
German: topographic name from a field name in North Rhine-Westphalia, denoting a sizeable piece of land, from Middle Low German dē ‘thigh’ (referring to the shape of the property).

nameMeanings.name: Sydenham
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of Family Names in Britain and Ireland © University of the West of England 2016
meanings: English: locative name from Sydenham in Bridgwater (Somerset), Sydenham Damerel (Devon), and Sydenham (Oxon).

nameMeanings.name: Matilda
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Latinized form of a Germanic personal name derived from maht, meht ‘might’ + hild ‘battle’. This was the name of an early German queen ( 895–968 ), wife of Henry the Fowler, who was noted for her piety and generosity. It was also the name of the wife of William the Conqueror and of the daughter of Henry I of England ( see Maud ). The name was introduced into England by the Normans, and this Latinized form is the one that normally occurs in medieval records, while the vernacular form Maud was the one in everyday use. Matilda was revived in England as a learned form in the 18th century.

variants.name: Mathilda
meanings: Latinized form of a Germanic personal name derived from maht, meht ‘might’ + hild ‘battle’. This was the name of an early German queen ( 895–968 ), wife of Henry the Fowler, who was noted for her piety and generosity. It was also the name of the wife of William the Conqueror and of the daughter of Henry I of England ( see Maud ). The name was introduced into England by the Normans, and this Latinized form is the one that normally occurs in medieval records, while the vernacular form Maud was the one in everyday use. Matilda was revived in England as a learned form in the 18th century.

en.lifeSummary: Matilda de Sydenham was born in 1238, in Sydenham, Somerset, England, United Kingdom as the daughter of Robert Walter de Sydenham. She had at least 1 son and 3 daughters with Andrew Jarpenville of Bushey. She died in 1270, in Drayton, Penkridge, Staffordshire, England, United Kingdom, at the age of 32.

Elle a eu au moins 1 fils et 3 filles avec Andrew Jarpenville of Bushey. Elle est décédée en 1270, à Drayton, Penkridge, Staffordshire, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni, à l’âge de 32 ans.

ja.lifeSummary: Matilda de Sydenhamは1238年にイギリスイギリスソメルセトシデンハムでRobert Walter de Sydenhamの娘として生まれた。彼女はAndrew Jarpenville of Busheyとの間に少なくとも1人の息子と3人の娘をもうけた。彼女は1270年にイギリスイギリススタフォードシュアイアペンクリッジドレイトンで32歳で死去した。

ko.lifeSummary: Matilda de Sydenham(은)는 1238년 영국 잉글랜드 서머세트 시데함에서 Robert Walter de Sydenham의 딸로 태어났다. 그녀는 Andrew Jarpenville of Bushey(와)과 적어도 한 명의 아들과 세 명의 딸을 두었다. 그녀는 1270년 영국 잉글랜드 스태퍼드실르 펜크리지 드라이턴에서 32세의 나이로 사망했다.
